news_summary: Astronomers have discovered two exceptionally light and rare super puff planets, showcasing remarkable diversity in planetary formation.
news_summary_fr: Des astronomes ont découvert deux planètes « super-puff » exceptionnellement légères et rares, qui témoignent d'une diversité remarquable dans la formation planétaire.
news_summary_es: Los astrónomos han descubierto dos «superplanetas hinchados» excepcionalmente ligeros y poco comunes, lo que pone de manifiesto la notable diversidad en la formación planetaria.

articles.title: Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
articles.summary: Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.
articles.content:
Astronomers have identified two of the fluffiest giant planets ever discovered, with densities so low they are actually less dense than cotton candy. The rare pair of "super-puff" planets was found by an international team led by the University of Oxford, working with Université Côte d'Azur/Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur and the University of Birmingham. The findings were published in Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

The newly confirmed planets, TOI-791 b and TOI-791 c, orbit an F7-type dwarf star about 1,110 light years from Earth in the southern constellation Volans. Although each planet is about the size of Jupiter, both are remarkably lightweight for their size.

TOI-791 b has a density of just 0.038 grams per cubic centimeter, while TOI-791 c measures 0.047 grams per cubic centimeter. Jupiter, by comparison, has an average density of 1.33 grams per cubic centimeter, making it roughly 28 to 35 times denser than these newly discovered worlds.

The comparison becomes even more striking when measured against candy floss, which has a typical density of about 0.05 grams per cubic centimeter. Earth is much denser still, averaging 5.5 grams per cubic centimeter.

Rare Planetary Twins Locked in a Gravitational Dance

Scientists believe the two planets formed together from the same disc of gas and dust surrounding their young star, making them planetary "siblings."

They are also linked by an unusual orbital arrangement called a 5:3 mean-motion resonance. For every five orbits completed by the inner planet, the outer planet finishes almost exactly three. As they circle their star, their gravity repeatedly pulls on one another, creating small but measurable changes in the timing of each planet's transit.

Only four other planetary systems are known to contain multiple super-puff planets, making TOI-791 an exceptionally rare opportunity to investigate how these unusual worlds originate and evolve.

Lead author Dr. George Dransfield (she/her) (Department of Physics, University of Oxford and a presenter for BBC Sky at Night) said:

"Only a handful of these super-puffy planets are known, and it is even rarer to find two in the same system. Their extremely low densities make them fascinating targets for understanding how planetary systems form and evolve."

Citizen Scientists Helped Find the Planets

Volunteers participating in the Planet Hunters TESS citizen-science project first flagged TOI-791 b in 2019 and TOI-791 c in 2023 as possible planets. The project searches observations collected by NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) for signs of previously unknown worlds.

Researchers then combined measurements from telescopes around the world to determine the planets' sizes and masses, allowing them to calculate their exceptionally low densities.

When a planet crosses in front of its star during a "transit," it blocks a small amount of starlight. That dip in brightness reveals the planet's size. In the TOI-791 system, astronomers also detected tiny changes in the timing of the transits caused by the planets' gravitational interactions. Analyzing those timing variations allowed the team to estimate each planet's mass.

Antarctica Played a Key Role

The discovery was built on eight years of observations, including data from the ASTEP (Antarctic Search for Transiting ExoPlanets) telescope at Concordia Station in Antarctica. The telescope is jointly operated by researchers from Université Côte d'Azur/Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur and international collaborators.

Antarctica's long winter nights gave astronomers a major advantage. Months of uninterrupted darkness made it possible to observe the planets' unusually long transits, each lasting more than 11 hours, without interruption. According to the researchers, these are the longest continuous planetary transits ever fully observed from the ground.

How Do Super-Puff Planets Form?

Scientists are still trying to understand how super-puff planets develop.

One leading explanation is that these worlds possess enormous atmospheres rich in hydrogen and helium that account for a large fraction of their total mass. Researchers think these thick gaseous envelopes may have formed when the planets were much farther from their star, in colder regions of the protoplanetary disc where gas could rapidly accumulate around a solid planetary core.

Future observations are planned to better understand the origins of these unusual planets and test competing theories.

Professor Amaury Triaud (University of Birmingham), the UK Principal Investigator of ASTEP and co-author of the study, said:

"This system offers a unique laboratory for understanding how super-puff planets form and evolve. We propose to carry out space-based observations using the James Webb Space Telescope to assess if the puffy atmosphere contains carbon-, nitrogen-, and oxygen-bearing species, revealing new insight into how these unusual planets formed."

Professor Tristan Guillot (Université Côte d'Azur), Principal Investigator of ASTEP and co-author of the study, added:

"These multi-planetary systems are complex, with gravitational interactions between the planets that evolve over very long periods, tens of years or more. This discovery highlights the importance of continued international collaboration in astronomy. Bringing together observations from Antarctica, space telescopes and observatories across several continents was essential to revealing the true nature of these extraordinary planets."
articles.reasoning: The discovery of two rare super-puff planets with extremely low densities provides significant new insights into planetary formation and evolution, representing a meaningful scientific breakthrough with broad implications for astronomy. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights international collaboration and citizen science contributions, making it inspiring and informative for the general public.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers discover two giant planets lighter than cotton candy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Astronomers study planets beyond our solar system to understand how they form and change. Some planets, called "super-puffs," are very large but surprisingly light, which puzzles scientists.

**What happened** – A team led by the University of Oxford found two super-puff planets named TOI-791 b and TOI-791 c, located about 1,110 light years away. Both are about the size of Jupiter but have densities even lower than cotton candy. These planets orbit their star in a special pattern where the inner planet circles five times while the outer one circles three times, a rare arrangement. The discovery involved data from NASA’s TESS satellite, telescopes worldwide, and special observations from Antarctica, where long nights allowed continuous watching of the planets.

**Impact** – These two super-puff planets are among the fluffiest ever found, with densities 28 to 35 times lower than Jupiter. Their unusual size and lightness help scientists learn how planets with huge, gas-rich atmospheres form and evolve. Because they orbit together in a rare resonance, studying them can reveal how planetary systems develop over time. The discovery also shows how citizen scientists and international cooperation can contribute to major findings.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use the James Webb Space Telescope to study the planets’ atmospheres more closely. They want to find out what gases they contain and test ideas about how super-puffs form, especially whether their thick hydrogen and helium layers formed far from their star before moving closer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two giant, extremely light planets orbiting a distant star offer a rare chance to understand how unusual worlds with giant gas envelopes come to be.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des astronomes découvrent deux planètes géantes plus légères que la barbe à papa
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les astronomes étudient des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire pour comprendre comment elles se forment et évoluent. Certaines planètes, appelées "super-pouff", sont très grandes mais étonnamment légères, ce qui intrigue les scientifiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par l'université d'Oxford a découvert deux planètes super-pouff nommées TOI-791 b et TOI-791 c, situées à environ 1 110 années-lumière. Elles ont la taille de Jupiter mais une densité encore plus faible que la barbe à papa. Ces planètes tournent autour de leur étoile selon un schéma spécial où la planète intérieure fait cinq orbites pendant que l'extérieur en fait trois, un arrangement rare. La découverte a utilisé des données du satellite TESS de la NASA, des télescopes dans le monde entier et des observations en Antarctique, où les longues nuits ont permis de les observer sans interruption.

**Impact** – Ces deux super-pouff sont parmi les plus légères jamais trouvées, avec des densités 28 à 35 fois inférieures à celle de Jupiter. Leur taille et leur légèreté inhabituelles aident les scientifiques à comprendre comment des planètes avec des atmosphères épaisses en gaz se forment et évoluent. Leur orbite en résonance rare permet d'étudier comment les systèmes planétaires se développent dans le temps. Cette découverte montre aussi comment les citoyens scientifiques et la coopération internationale peuvent mener à des résultats importants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser le télescope spatial James Webb pour étudier plus en détail l'atmosphère des planètes. Ils veulent savoir quels gaz elles contiennent et tester des idées sur la formation des super-pouff, notamment si leurs couches épaisses d'hydrogène et d'hélium se sont formées loin de leur étoile avant de se rapprocher.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Deux planètes géantes et extrêmement légères orbitant une étoile lointaine offrent une chance rare de comprendre comment se forment des mondes inhabituels avec de grandes enveloppes gazeuses.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos descubren dos planetas gigantes más livianos que el algodón de azúcar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los astrónomos estudian planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar para entender cómo se forman y cambian con el tiempo. Algunos planetas llamados "superinflados" son muy grandes pero sorprendentemente livianos, lo que desconcierta a los científicos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por la Universidad de Oxford encontró dos planetas superinflados llamados TOI-791 b y TOI-791 c, ubicados a unos 1,110 años luz de distancia. Ambos tienen el tamaño de Júpiter pero densidades más bajas que el algodón de azúcar. Estos planetas orbitan su estrella en un patrón especial donde el planeta interior da cinco vueltas mientras el exterior da tres, una relación rara. El hallazgo usó datos del satélite TESS de la NASA, telescopios en todo el mundo y observaciones desde la Antártida, donde las largas noches permitieron observarlos sin interrupciones.

**Impacto** – Estos dos planetas superinflados están entre los más livianos encontrados, con densidades 28 a 35 veces menores que Júpiter. Su tamaño y ligereza inusuales ayudan a los científicos a aprender cómo se forman y evolucionan planetas con atmósferas grandes y llenas de gas. Debido a su rara resonancia orbital, estudiarlos puede revelar cómo se desarrollan los sistemas planetarios. El descubrimiento también muestra cómo los científicos ciudadanos y la cooperación internacional pueden contribuir a grandes hallazgos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar el telescopio espacial James Webb para estudiar mejor las atmósferas de los planetas. Quieren descubrir qué gases contienen y probar ideas sobre cómo se forman los superinflados, especialmente si sus gruesas capas de hidrógeno y helio se formaron lejos de su estrella antes de acercarse.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Dos planetas gigantes y extremadamente livianos que orbitan una estrella distante ofrecen una oportunidad rara para entender cómo se forman mundos inusuales con grandes envolturas gaseosas.
articles.image_prompt: Two enormous, translucent, cotton candy–like planets gently orbiting a softly glowing star, their delicate, wispy atmospheres swirling lightly as they dance in a precise cosmic rhythm, set against a serene deep blue space background with subtle hints of Antarctic ice and a faint constellation outline.
